Studies on the applications, energetics, and mechanisms of
charge-remote fragmentations are reviewed, with emphasis
given to those articles published after 1992. Independent of the
charge status, charge-remote fragmentations are analogous to
gas-phase thermolysis. Under collisional activation and with a
®xed charge, ions containing long-chain or poly-ring structures
undergo charge-remote fragmentations, generating product ions
that are structurally informative. Interpretation of the product-
ion spectra enables one to elucidate molecular structures.
Although charge-remote fragmentations have been successfully
used in the structural determination of fatty acids, phospholi-
pids, glycolipids, triacylglycerols, steroids, peptides, ceramides,
and other systems, the energetics and mechanisms of these reac-
tions are still debated because none of the existing mechanisms
can explain all the experimental data. ß 2000 John Wiley &

I. INTRODUCTION
Charge-remote fragmentation is a class of gas-phase
decompositions that occur physically remote from the
charge site (Adams, 1990; Gross, 1992; Adams & Songer,
1993). The reactions, analogous to gas-phase thermolysis
(Adams & Gross, 1989), are independent of the charge
status because there appears to be no important interac-
tion between reaction sites and the charge. Under high-
energy collisional activation (CA), charge-remote frag-

 mentations always take place along a fatty-acid chain no
matter how the fatty acid is charged. The precursor ion can
be a [M ÿ H  2Met] ion (Met  Li, Na, K, Rb,
or Cs) (Adams & Gross, 1987), a [M  Metÿ H] ion
(Met  Mg2, Ca2, Sr2, or Ba2) (Davoli & Gross,
1990), a [M ÿ H]ÿ ion (Tomer, Jensen & Gross, 1986a), or
the [M  H] ion of a suitably derivatized acid (Deterding
& Gross, 1987, 1988).
To assure that charge-remote fragmentation is the
predominant reaction channel, it is important to ®x the
charge, preferentially at one end of the molecular chain. If
the charge is not ®xed, then charge-driven reactions com-
pete with charge-remote fragmentation, generating pro-
duct-ion mixtures and resulting in spectra that are dif®cult
to interpret. For example, CA of the [M ÿ H  2Li] ions
of fatty acids gives exclusively product ions from charge-
remote fragmentation because the two lithium ions are
tightly bound to the carboxylate. On the other hand, the
[M  H] ion of the same fatty acid does not undergo
charge-remote fragmentation because the initial reaction
is H2O loss from the precursor, facilitating charge migra-
tion onto the alkyl chain and favoring charge-driven pro-
cesses (Adams & Gross, 1987).
High-energy CA always favors charge-remote frag-
mentation, although sometimes these reactions do occur
under low-energy CA or even as a metastable-ion process.
The energy supplied to the precursor ion by collisional
activation plays an important role in these decomposition
processes. An ion possessing a long alkyl chain with a
®xed charge always undergoes charge-remote fragmenta-
tions, provided that suf®cient energy is deposited in it.
Therefore, the ionization method is not a prerequisite for
charge-remote fragmentations. Although fast atom bom-
bardment (FAB) has been the most frequently used ioni-
zation method to generate precursor ions for charge-
remote fragmentations, electron ionization (EI), chemical
ionization (CI), ®eld desorption (FD), electrospray ioniza-
tion (ESI), and matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization
(MALDI) can also be utilized.
Although it has been established that charge-remote
fragmentation is analogous to gas-phase thermal reactions
of neutral long-chain molecules, the detailed mechanisms
and energetics of these reactions are still debated. Jensen,
Tomer and Gross (1985a) proposed that charge-remote
fragmentation is a concerted reaction that involves a peri-
cyclic mechanism and a six-membered ring transition
state. The product ions from this mechanism are a hydro-
gen molecule, a neutral terminal alkene, and a charged
terminal alkene (Equation 1). On the other hand, Wysocki
et al. (1988), and Wysocki and Ross (1991) proposed a
two-step mechanism initiated by a C±C bond cleavage,
generating two terminal free radicals that each sub-
sequently loses a radical to give the observed products
(Equation 2). Other mechanisms were also proposed
around 1990 (McAdoo, 1988; Contado et al., 1991), but
none of these mechanisms can explain all the experi-
mental data.
Studies that addressed charge-remote fragmentation
and that were published before 1992 were thoroughly
reviewed by Adams (1990) and by Gross (1992). This
review focuses on recent developments (since 1992) in
charge-remote fragmentation, its applications in structural
elucidation, and its energetics and mechanisms.
II. APPLICATIONS
Charge-remote fragmentation has been proven useful in
the structural determination of long-chain or poly-ring
molecules. Since 1983 (Tomer, Crow & Gross, 1983),
structures of a variety of synthetic and natural molecules,
including fatty acids and their derivatives, glyceropho-
spholipids, glycolipids, ceramides, carbohydrates, anti-
biotics, steroids, and peptides, have been determined by
charge-remotefragmentations(Adams,1990; Gross,1992).
These results will be summarized in this section.
A. Fatty Acids and Their Derivatives
Under CA, the [M ÿ H], or the [M ÿ H  2Met]
(Met  alkali metal ions), or the [M  Met] (Met  alka-
line earth metal ions) ions of a fatty acid undergo charge-
remote fragmentation to give a series of product ions
from which one can determine completely the fatty-acid
structure. Ions of molecules consisting of straight, unsub-
stituted alkyl chains fragment by losses of CnH2n2 or
CnH2n1, generating an array of product ions separated
by 14 u. An interruption in the alkyl chain causes the
formation of speci®c product ions that allow one to deter-
mine the nature and location of the functional group on
the chain. Locations of double bond, alkyl, hydroxyl,
epoxy, and cyclopropane/cyclopropene substitutions on
fatty-acid chains can be differentiated by peak patterns of
corresponding ions (Tomer, Crow & Gross, 1983; Tomer,
Jensen & Gross, 1986a; Jensen, Tomer & Gross, 1985b;

A. Fatty Acids and Their Derivatives
Under CA, the [M ÿ H], or the [M ÿ H  2Met]
(Met  alkali metal ions), or the [M  Met] (Met  alka-
line earth metal ions) ions of a fatty acid undergo charge-
remote fragmentation to give a series of product ions
from which one can determine completely the fatty-acid
structure. Ions of molecules consisting of straight, unsub-
stituted alkyl chains fragment by losses of CnH2n2 or
CnH2n1, generating an array of product ions separated
by 14 u. An interruption in the alkyl chain causes the
formation of speci®c product ions that allow one to deter-
mine the nature and location of the functional group on
the chain. Locations of double bond, alkyl, hydroxyl,
epoxy, and cyclopropane/cyclopropene substitutions on
fatty-acid chains can be differentiated by peak patterns of
corresponding ions (Tomer, Crow & Gross, 1983; Tomer,
Jensen & Gross, 1986a; Jensen, Tomer & Gross, 1985b;
Adams & Gross, 1986, 1987, 1989; Davoli & Gross,
1990; Crockett et al., 1990; Bernstrom, Kayganich &
Murphy, 1991; Wheelan, Zirrolli & Murphy, 1993).
Similarly, structures of alkylamines, fatty alcohols, and
fatty-acid esters, and related materials can be determined
(Shimomura et al., 1993; Claeys & van den Heuvel, 1994;
Calas et al., 1997; Li et al., 1997; Suma, Raju &
Vairamani, 1997; Li & Liu, 1998).
In a collaborative application of charge-remote frag-
mentation, Fu et al. (1997) addressed the structural deter-
mination of six new acetylenic compounds, pellynic acid,
pellynol A-D, and pellynone. These lipid-like materials
were isolated from the marine sponge Pellina triangulata.
Their structures, which had a polar alcohol function at
both ends, were established using nuclear magnetic
resonance (NMR) spectroscopy and chemical degrada-
tion. We then applied tandem mass spectrometry of the
lithium adducts of the acetylenic compounds to con®rm
the structural assignments and to ascertain whether
information regarding the site of internal saturation in
these polyfunctional compounds. Indeed it did, except for
the unknowns that contained a keto (oxo) group. The
inability to locate the keto group is not a limitation of
charge-remote chemistry but rather stemmed from our
ignorance of the charge-remote fragmentation around a
keto group. To correct this limitation, we designed a
systematic study of fatty acids that contained this
Using high-energy CA, Cheng, Giblin and Gross
(1998) studied the fragmentation of a set of fatty acids that
contain the keto group. Some characteristic product ions
are formed by cleavages near the keto group: on the side
proximal to the charge, three product ions (a, b, and g
ions) are formed by a, b, and g cleavages, respectively,
whereas on the side distal to the charge, only one product
ion is generated by g0 cleavage. The location of the keto
group in an oxofatty acid can be easily determined by
identifying peaks that correspond to these ions and to the
gap in its CAD spectrum (Fig. 1). The CAD spectrum of
the [M ÿ H  2Li] ion of 12-oxooctadecanoic acid has a
gap after the peak that corresponds to the m/z 197 ion and
two high-intensity peaks that correspond to b and g0 ions
at m/z 183 and 253, respectively, whereas that of 13-
oxooctadecanoic acid gives a gap after the peak that
corresponds to the m/z 211 ion and two high-intensity
peaks corresponds to b and g0 ions at m/z 197 and 267,
respectively. This is one example of many that shows
that oxofatty acid isomers can be readily distinguished
Low-energy CA can also cause some charge-remote
fragmentation, although a complete array of product ions
such as those seen in Fig. 1 is seldom obtained. Whalen,
Grossert and Boyd (1995) observed that ions of charge-
remote fragmentation could be initiated in quadrupole
collision cells where the collision energy is below 100 eV.
Recently, Hsu and Turk (1999) produced a nearly complete
array of ions from charge-remote fragmentation with a
triple-stage quadrupole instrument, permitting assignment
of double-bond locations in an alkenyl chain. With a
collisional energy of 400 eV, Grif®ths et al. (1996)
observed charge-remote fragmentations that are compar-
able to those occurring when the collision energy is in the
keV range (laboratory frame). When the elucidation of
only the functional groups in fatty acids is sought, low-
energy CA can provide suf®cient information. Wheelan,
Zirrolli and Murphy (1996) investigated the decomposi-
tion of an important lipid mediator, leukotriene B4 [5(S),
12(R)-dihydroxy-6(Z),8(E),14(Z)-eicosatetraenoic
(LTB4)] and its metabolites, by ESI with low-energy
CA. Although the structures of (LTB4) (1) and its
metabolites are complicated, CA of the [M ÿ H]ÿ ion of
each molecule generates structurally informative product
ions that reveal the nature of the alkyl chain (the number
and locations of double bonds and hydroxyl groups).

To make the use of charge-remote fragmentation more
compatible with on-line separation of fatty-acid mixture
analysis, derivatization of the carboxyl group is needed.
Early examples of derivatization include the introduction
of the triphenylphosphonium group by Watson and co-
workers (Chang & Watson, 1992; Chang, Gage & Watson,

There is some controversy about the energetics and
mechanisms of charge-remote fragmentation, even though
many research groups have successfully used these reac-
tions to elucidate structures of a variety of synthetic
and natural molecules. The 1,4-elimination mechanism
(Jensen, Tomer & Gross, 1985a) was the ®rst proposal
FIGURE 7. The EI-MS/MS spectrum of the M ion of a-tocopherol. (Reprinted with permission from J
Mass Spectrom 1995, 30:1480±1488. Copyright 1995, John Wiley & Sons.)
to explain the reactions taking place on the alkyl chain.
The mechanism is supported by molecular orbital
calculations (Siegel & Colthup, 1988) because it has a
lower activation energy than that of homolytic cleavage.
Formation of some product ions, however, cannot be ex-
plained by the mechanism. An example is the formation
of the product ion by CH4 loss (unlikely to be CH2 and
H2). Product ions that are radical cations or anions form-
ed near the positive or negative charge are undoubtedly
formed by homolytic cleavages because they are open-
shell species. The homolytic-cleavage mechanism, on the
other hand, also has some drawbacks. The high activation
energy should make it dif®cult for the pathway to
compete effectively with concerted mechanisms. If the
reaction is indeed a two-step process and the formation of
the two diradicals generated by homolytic C±C bond
cleavage is the rate-determining step, then there should
be no isotope effect. Nevertheless, an isotope effect
should pertain to the 1,4-elimination whether the H is
transferred toward or away from the double bond. This
reaction, however, is not consistent with recent experi-
mental results (to be discussed later).

The 1,4-elimination mechanism suggests that two C±H
bonds and one C±C bond break nearly simultaneously,

Denekamp et al. (2000) showed in another study the
unusual isotope effect for cleavage along an alkyl chain.
For three alkyl cations (i.e., n-hexadecylpyridinium, n-
hexadecyltriphenylphosphonium, and n-hexadecyltriethyl-
ammonium), a small isotope effect (1.3) pertained for
cleavage along the alkyl chain that contained ±CD2, but
only when the product contains ±CD±±CH2. The observed
``one-way isotope effect'' is inconsistent with the peri-
cyclic mechanism, and the authors interpret it as evidence
for a C±H bond cleavage as the rate-determining step. A
problem with the interpretation is that the departing
neutral would be a radical, at least initially, but the neutral
is known to be an alkene (Cordero & Wesdemiotis, 1994).
Furthermore, the initially formed charged product, accord-
ing to the mechanism where C±H bond cleavage occurs,
would be a distonic ion that would fragment by loss of a
second H atom. We think the more likely reaction of a
distonic product is a radical-triggered loss of ethene.

interactions occur between the charge site and the reaction
center via coiled conformations. We would not deny the
possibility of coiling; the issue is whether that interaction

Busch and coworkers (Carlson & Busch, 1994; Melnyk
et al., 1998) used carbocyanine dyes as molecular probes
to study the energetics of charge-remote fragmentation.
As exempli®ed by structure 23, these compounds contain
two long alkyl chains and a resonance-stabilized charge
site. Experimental results show that two distinct charge-
remote processes occur, depending on the energy supplied
to the precursor ion. Under low-energy CA, the precursor
ion loses the entire ®rst alkyl chain followed by charge-
remote fragmentation along the second alkyl chain. The
relative abundances of ions from charge-remote frag-
mentation can be manipulated by collision energy: the
higher the collision energy, the more abundant the product
ions. With suf®cient collision energy, product ions from
charge-remote fragmentation on the ®rst alkyl chain
appear and ®nally become dominant. The authors con-
cluded that the charge-remote fragmentation on the
second chain is a low-energy process, whereas that on
the ®rst chain is high-energy (Melnyk et al., 1998).
Cheng, Pittenauer and Gross (1998) observed an
energy effect that may be contradictory. The [M  Na]
ions of triacylglycerols with lower internal energy
(produced by ESI) undergo high-energy, homolytic cleav-
ages to generate ions by CnH2n1 losses. Those with higher
average internal energy (produced by FAB), however,
react through the low-energy 1,4-H2 elimination process
to give ions by CnH2n2 losses (Fig. 10). The authors
suggested an explanation on the basis of the internal-
energy distribution, r(E), of the precursor ions. After
collisional activation, a small portion of the relatively
``cold'' ESI-produced precursor ions has been promoted
to the ``long-tail'' high-energy region, permitting simple
cleavages and rearrangements to both take place, with the
former as the major pathway. On the other hand, the FAB-
produced ions contain initially higher internal energy, and
collisional activation pushes a larger fraction of the
distribution into the region for rearrangement. Although a
long-tail region is populated once again by collisional
activation, the relative number of ions with internal
energies in the high-energy tail is small and, therefore,
fewer product ions are formed by simple cleavage. Another
explanation, likely to be favored by Claeys and coworkers,
is that the reactions of the FAB-produced ions begin with
the higher energy C±H homolytic cleavage, whereas those
of ESI-produced ions are initiated by the lower energy
C±C cleavages. A third is that the pericyclic reaction is a
higher energy process than that initiated by a homolytic
C±C or C±H bond cleavage, but this is unlikely.
Voinov and Claeys (2000) turned up additional
evidence in favor of a rate-determining C±H cleavage
for reactions along a saturated alkyl chain. In a clever
strategy, they produced by resonant electron capture (7.2
eV) an [M ÿ H]ÿ of a fatty acid in which the H had been
abstracted from the alkyl chain. Remarkably, these species
show the ``classic'' charge-remote pattern for fatty acids.
The introduction of 7.2 eV of internal excitation is not
suf®cient to promote charge-remote fragmentation if the
energy is delocalized prior to fragmentation, as is the main
tenet of the quasi-equilibrium theory of mass spectro-

fragmentation is ``nonergodic,'' possibly explaining that
high-energy processes can occur by the deposition of
energy at localized sites. Interestingly, they also found that
at lower capture energies, the principal fragmentation was
loss of radicals, as we had found when we compared the
results of high-energy CA on FAB- and ESI-produced
precursor ions (Cheng, Pittenauer & Gross, 1998).
Charge-remote fragmentation is an important concept
that governs gas-phase reactions, and has important
applications in the structural determination of long-chain
and poly-ring molecules. With a ®xed charge (inherent or
through ionization), a variety of synthetic and natural
molecules, ranging from fatty acids to complex lipids,
appropriate activation, generating structurally informative
Although fundamental studies have provided ion
chemists with some information about the energetics and
mechanism of charge-remote fragmentations, a complete
understanding of these reactions awaits further investiga-
tions. Certainly Claeys and co-workers have recently pro-
duced intriguing results in favor of the concept that C±H
bond cleavage is rate-determining. Their interpretations
probably won't go unchallenged, however, because they
appear to violate Occam's razor. Perhaps more than one
mechanism is needed to explain all the experimental data
because the reactions are undoubtedly complicated, espe-
cially when functional groups on chain or ring systems
participate in the decomposition of the precursor ion, but
simplicity is preferred in the absence of a ®nal answer.
Nevertheless, charge-remote fragmentation forms an
important intellectual basis for gas-phase ion chemistry,
allowing scientists to classify and organize gas-phase
processes as charge-driven, radical-driven (if appropriate),
charge-remote, or occurring via ion-neutral complexes.
